summary: Training simulation on critical decisions during the Costa Concordia casualty.

checkpoints.title: Unauthorized deviation detected
checkpoints.summary: The inchino toward Isola del Giglio is ordered outside the approved route and with unvalidated references.

twinAlerts.title: Route deviation
twinAlerts.detail: The actual track no longer matches the approved voyage plan.

twinAlerts.title: Insufficient BRM control
twinAlerts.detail: No one on the bridge is stopping a clearly unsafe decision.

lawOverlay.title: Voyage planning
lawOverlay.detail: The track must be planned, documented, and followed.

lawOverlay.title: Shipboard operations
lawOverlay.detail: The company must ensure navigation and control procedures.

decision.prompt: What operational order would have reduced risk at this point?

options.label: Continue the inchino
options.outcome: An unapproved maneuver is maintained with ever-smaller margins.

options.label: Abort and return to the planned route
options.outcome: Navigation control is restored before entering the risk zone.

options.label: Reduce speed but keep the approach
options.outcome: It reduces part of the risk, but the unauthorized deviation remains.

debrief.title: The failure begins before the impact
debrief.summary: The casualty does not start with the collision, but with an out-of-procedure route decision that nobody corrects.
lessons: The voyage plan is not decorative; it is a technical and legal barrier.
BRM must allow officers to challenge the captain when safety is falling.
Reducing speed helps, but it does not correct an unauthorized maneuver.
debrief.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Approved route] --> B{Captain's decision}
  B -->|Abort inchino| C[Return to safe route]
  B -->|Keep deviation| D[Accumulated risk]
  D --> E[Insufficient maneuver margin]

checkpoints.title: Impact and loss of propulsion
checkpoints.summary: The hull strikes Le Scole, a major breach opens, and propulsion power is lost.

twinAlerts.title: Loss of structural integrity
twinAlerts.detail: The extent of damage exceeds the ordinary operating scenario.

twinAlerts.title: Progressive flooding risk
twinAlerts.detail: Loss of watertight integrity compromises stability and essential systems.

lawOverlay.title: Damage stability
lawOverlay.detail: The ship must maintain survivability and effective subdivision.

lawOverlay.title: Pollution risk
lawOverlay.detail: The casualty creates an immediate fuel leakage scenario.

decision.prompt: After the impact, what is the correct first critical decision?

options.label: Report only an electrical failure
options.outcome: Crew and passengers lose critical time without understanding the real gravity.

options.label: Activate emergency and assess real damage
options.outcome: Operational time is gained for control, compartmentalization, and evacuation preparation.

options.label: Wait for full confirmation before alarming
options.outcome: It looks prudent, but it degrades the response window.

debrief.title: Lost time becomes unrecoverable
debrief.summary: When the system is already physically degraded, hiding the severity accelerates operational collapse.
lessons: A blackout after impact is not a minor issue; it must be treated as an emergency until major damage is ruled out.
An early emergency signal organizes resources; it does not create panic by itself.
Useful response time is measured in minutes, not intuition.
debrief.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Impact 21:42] --> B{Initial response}
  B -->|Immediate emergency| C[Diagnosis and control]
  B -->|Minimize damage| D[Operational delay]
  D --> E[More difficult evacuation]

checkpoints.title: Coast Guard requests the real situation
checkpoints.summary: Livorno contacts the ship while passengers are already raising the alarm on their own.

twinAlerts.title: False operational narrative
twinAlerts.detail: The blackout version contradicts passenger calls and the ship's physical reality.

twinAlerts.title: Degraded SAR coordination
twinAlerts.detail: Without truthful information, external assets cannot escalate in time.

lawOverlay.title: Emergency communication
lawOverlay.detail: The emergency must be reported quickly and without falsifying the situation.

lawOverlay.title: Danger reporting
lawOverlay.detail: Maritime authorities must receive reliable operational information.

decision.prompt: What communication was correct at this point?

options.label: Declare flooding and loss of control
options.outcome: It allows SAR to coordinate real support and accelerates evacuation preparation.

options.label: Maintain the blackout version
options.outcome: External assistance is delayed and bridge authority erodes.

options.label: Report only moderate list
options.outcome: It improves coordination somewhat, but still hides the critical severity of the damage.

debrief.title: Communication is also a safety maneuver
debrief.summary: Lying or minimizing during a maritime emergency destroys coordination capacity and increases final damage.
lessons: SAR needs operational truth, not reputational protection.
If passengers are reporting better than the bridge, command is already degraded.
External coordination must start before the chaotic phase, not after.
debrief.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[SAR requests status] --> B{Message sent}
  B -->|Real status| C[Early coordination]
  B -->|Blackout| D[SAR delay]
  D --> E[Evacuation without sufficient support]

checkpoints.title: Abandon ship with a 1h 12m delay
checkpoints.summary: The order comes with severe list, port-side boats unusable, and passengers already disorganized.

twinAlerts.title: Late evacuation
twinAlerts.detail: The ship's angle already invalidates part of the abandonment equipment.

twinAlerts.title: Insufficient training
twinAlerts.detail: Without drill or clear orders, the flow of people becomes chaotic.

lawOverlay.title: Operation of life-saving appliances
lawOverlay.detail: Boat operations require time, control, and still-recoverable conditions.

lawOverlay.title: Crisis management
lawOverlay.detail: Passenger vessel personnel must be able to control and guide passengers.

decision.prompt: Which decision would have produced a better result before reaching this point?

options.label: Order preparation and abandonment much earlier
options.outcome: Life-saving appliances would have operated with more margin and less list.

options.label: Wait until the moment it was actually ordered
options.outcome: The evacuation starts already degraded and with partial capability.

options.label: Prepare only the crew without informing passengers
options.outcome: It reduces some internal chaos, but does not correct the decisive delay.

debrief.title: A late evacuation is not fixed with stronger orders
debrief.summary: The greatest damage at this stage had already been planted by the prior delay in recognizing the emergency and organizing the response.
lessons: Abandon-ship preparation must start before the vessel loses operational geometry.
The absence of a drill worsens any real evacuation.
Time is not recovered with more verbal authority once physics has already won.
debrief.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Severe damage] --> B{Abandon ship order}
  B -->|Early| C[Usable appliances]
  B -->|Late| D[Extreme list]
  D --> E[Chaos and reduced capacity]
